coredata.dc:title: Preparation and properties of copper matrix composites synergistically strengthened by Al2O3 and CPD
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Haichuan
Bao, Rui
Ma, Ruofei
Liu, Jiachen
Nie, Youliang
Yi, Jianhong
coredata.dc:description: The development of copper-based composite materials is increasingly requiring both high electrical conductivity and better mechanical properties. In this paper, a novel copper-based composite was prepared by combining carbonized polymer dot (CPD) with flake copper powder (BMCu) and Al2O3 with molecular-level copper powder (MLCu). The composite obtained good mechanical properties with the yield strength of 157.00 MPa and the tensile strength of 366.14 MPa, which are increased by 17.4% and 25.1% compared with those of pure copper matrix (133.76 MPa and 292.72 MPa). The hardness of the composite is 124.7 HV, 27.4% higher than that of pure copper (97.9 HV), and the conductivity is 93.15% IACS. Besides, the strengthening mechanism of tensile property is also discussed, and the improvement of composite strength is mainly due to the dislocation strengthening. This study provides a new idea for the preparation of copper-based composite materials with high electrical conductivity and mechanical properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper-based composites
Al2O3
CPD
Synergistic strengthening

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of the CPD; (b) HRTEM of the CPD with crystalline carbon; (c) FFT and IFFT of the yellow dashed box in (b); (d) FFT and IFFT of the orange dashed box in (b); (e) HRTEM of the CPD with amorphous carbon; (f) XRD of Al2O3. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

SEM patterns of the prepared powder. (a) MLCu after reduction; (b) MLCPD before reduction; (c) MLCPD after reduction; (d) MLAl2O3 before reduction; (e) MLAl2O3 after reduction; (f) BMCu after reduction; (g) BMCPD after reduction; (h) BMAl2O3 after reduction; (i) MLAl2O3 + BMCPD after reduction.
Fracture surfaces of the tensile tested specimens. (a) Cu; (b) MLCPD + BMCPD; (c) MLAl2O3 + BMAl2O3; (d) BMCPD + BMAl2O3; (e) MLCPD + MLAl2O3; (f) MLCPD + BMAl2O3; (g) MLAl2O3 + BMCPD.

(a) TEM of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD; (b) EDS Mapping; (c) HRTEM of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD; (d) FFT and IFFT of the region (d) in (c); (e) FFT and IFFT of the region (e) in(c); (f) FFT and IFFT of the region (f) in (c).
(a) HRTEM image of the interfacial structure; (b) FFT and IFFT of the region (b) in (a); (c) FFT and IFFT of the region (c) in (a); (d1), (d2) FFT and IFFT of the region (d) in (a).
Metallographic picture and statistical histogram of average grain size. (a) Cu; (b) MLAl2O3 + BMCPD.
(a) XRD of Cu and MLAl2O3 + BMCPD after tensile deformation; (b) Williamson-Hall scatter diagram drawn by (a).
Calculated and experimental values of tensile strength and reinforcement contribution of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD composite.

head.ce:title: Preparation and properties of copper matrix composites synergistically strengthened by Al2O3 and CPD

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650031, Yunnan, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650031, Yunnan, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of copper-based composite materials is increasingly requiring both high electrical conductivity and better mechanical properties. In this paper, a novel copper-based composite was prepared by combining carbonized polymer dot (CPD) with flake copper powder (BMCu) and Al2O3 with molecular-level copper powder (MLCu). The composite obtained good mechanical properties with the yield strength of 157.00 MPa and the tensile strength of 366.14 MPa, which are increased by 17.4% and 25.1% compared with those of pure copper matrix (133.76 MPa and 292.72 MPa). The hardness of the composite is 124.7 HV, 27.4% higher than that of pure copper (97.9 HV), and the conductivity is 93.15% IACS. Besides, the strengthening mechanism of tensile property is also discussed, and the improvement of composite strength is mainly due to the dislocation strengthening. This study provides a new idea for the preparation of copper-based composite materials with high electrical conductivity and mechanical properties.

ce:section.ce:para: Numerous studies indicate that ceramics as reinforcement can improve the mechanical properties of metal materials, such as Al2O3 [1–4], SiC [5,6], SiO2 [7] and TiB2 [8]. Unfortunately, the electrical conductivity and elongation of the composite materials are deteriorated when these ceramics are employed [1,9], resulting in limited applications of the related composites. In comparison, nano-carbon reinforcements spring out due to their outstanding tensile strength and excellent electrical conductivity, just like carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene [10,11]. However, nano-carbon tends to agglomerate and forms tiny defects in composites, and this dispersion problem would impair mechanical properties and electrical conductivity [12,13]. Very recently, some reports suggest that this problem can be effectively solved by using 0-dimensional nano-carbon. Elizaveta et al. [14] prepared 30 nm fullerene soot nano-particles to enhance copper, increasing the hardness of the composite material. Zhao et al. [15] obtain the copper-based composite with 98% IACS conductivity, 262 MPa tensile strength and 39% elongation using 2.20 nm CPD as reinforcement. These studies clearly prove that 0-dimensional nano-carbon overcomes the agglomeration problem of nano-carbon. However, due to the few studies on the addition of 0-dimensional nano-carbon into metal as reinforcement, the role of 0-dimensional nano-carbon has not been fully played in current studies, and further studies are needed.

In addition, some researchers begin to add nano-carbon and ceramics to copper matrix as hybrid reinforcement. Yu et al. [16] produced Al2O3 and CNTs to gain the composite with 345 MPa ultimate tensile strength and 87.2% IACS conductivity, and the ultimate tensile strength increased by 73.4% compared with pure copper. Fei et al. [17] used TiB2 and CNTs to synergistically strengthen copper. After hot extrusion, the sample showed an 86.3% increase in tensile strength compared with that of pure copper, and the elongation reached 32.4%. Hence, the hybrid of nano-carbon and ceramics is a feasible and useful strategy to strengthen copper-based composite materials.

Meanwhile, the mixing methods of reinforcements and metal matrix also affect the properties of the composites. Molecular-level method and ball milling method are common mixing methods. The molecular-level method is usually used to solve the agglomeration problem of CNTs and graphene in metals [18,19]. Most of the composites prepared by this method have high tensile strength, but the elongation is poor due to the severe grain refinement of the matrix. Xu et al. [20] proposed the method of shift-speed ball milling. Compared with low-speed and high-speed ball milling, the tensile strength of the composite did not decrease, and the elongation increases by 2–3 times. Therefore, a combination of the two powder mixing methods can be considered to obtain composites with better tensile strength and elongation.

Based on the above researches, this experiment investigated the synergistic strengthening of copper by ceramic phase (Al2O3) and 0-dimensional nano-carbon (CPD). The pure Cu is a combination of molecular-level copper powder (MLCu) and ball-milled flake copper powder (BMCu). Al2O3 and CPD are combined with copper by different mixing methods.

Cu powders (99.9% purity, 325 meshes particle size, Shanghai Naiou Nano Science and Technology Co. LTD, China.), Al2O3 (99.99%, 10 nm, γ-phase, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. LTD, China.), Citric acid, Copper acetate monohydrate, Glucose anhydrase, Sodium hydroxide (AR, Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. LTD, China.) and Ethylene diamine anhydrous (AR, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory) were used as raw material for research.

Copper acetate monohydrate was dissolved in deionized water and stirred well, then placed in a 60 °C water bath. Then sodium hydroxide was added drop by drop and when the solution turned black, glucose was added drop by drop. The solution got from the reaction was filtered and dried to obtain the powder, which is then heated at 280 °C for 500 min in the atmosphere of 90% N2 + 10% H2 to gain MLCu finally.

The 325 mesh copper powder was milled in stainless steel ball mill tanks at 200 rpm for 8 h. The ball milling medium was alcohol, then milled at 400 rpm for 5 h. The milled powder was filtered, dried, and heated at 300 °C for 300 min in the atmosphere of 90% N2 + 10% H2 to get BMCu finally.

Added 12 g citric acid and 2.01 mL ethylene diamine anhydrous to 120 mL deionized water. After stirring evenly, the solution was transferred to an autoclave with Teflon lining and heated at 150 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was dialyzed for 30 h to obtain pure CPD aqueous solution. Finally, the colloidal solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain CPD powder.

In the preparation of MLCu, Al2O3 or CPD was added to the copper acetate solution, and then sodium hydroxide and glucose were added. Thus, MLAl2O3 or MLCPD was prepared. Similarly, in the preparation of BMCu, Al2O3 or CPD was added to the ball milling tank together with 325 mesh copper powder for subsequent ball milling to prepare BMAl2O3 or BMCPD. The mixing of BM sample and ML sample was also a ball milling process with no ball milling medium and dry milling at 200 rpm for 2 h. Finally, the composite powder was heated at 300 °C for 300 min in the atmosphere of 90% N2 + 10% H2. In all samples, the total amount of reinforcements was 0.45 wt%. In the synergistically strengthened samples, the ratio of CPD and Al2O3 was 1:1, and the ratio of BMCu and MLCu was also 1:1.

The prepared powder was sintered in a spark plasma sintering system (SPS), where the temperature was increased to 600 °C within 7 min and the pressure was maintained at 10 MPa, then the temperature was increased to 650 °C and the pressure was increased to 50 MPa within 1 min, and maintained at 650 °C, 50 MPa for 5 min. The whole sintering process was carried out under vacuum atmosphere, and finally a sample with a diameter of 20 mm was sintered.

The microstructure of CPD and bulk sample were observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2-TF300, S-Twin), the physical phase of the powder was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT, Rigaku, Japan, Cu-Kα radiation source), and the dislocation density of the samples was calculated. The microstructure of the powder and the fracture surfaces of the tensile tested specimens were observed by scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Nova nano-450, FEI, accelerating voltage during SEM investigations was 15 kV). The density is measured by Archimedes principle. The conductivity was measured by digital conductivity meter (Sigma, 2008b), and the tensile test was carried out on the sample by universal testing machine (Ag-x-100kN, Shimadzu).

Fig. 1a shows the microstructure of the CPD. It can be observed that the CPD owned a good spherical structure and a uniform dispersion. According to other studies, at least 200 particles are required for standard particle size distribution statistics [21]. According to the particle size statistics, the minimum particle size was 1.37 nm, the maximum particle size was 5.13 nm, and the average particle size was 2.96 nm. HRTEM images of CPD show two types of structures: one had good crystallinity and clear lattice stripes (Fig. 1b). FFT and IFFT transformations were done in the yellow dashed box to obtain Fig. 1c, which were determined by measuring their lattice spacing and angle as C(111) and C(200), corresponding to the standard PDF card numbered 80-0017. The FFT and IFFT transformations were done in the orange dashed box in Fig. 1b to obtain Fig. 1d, and C(100) was found to be consistent with the standard PDF card numbered 80–0004. The other one was amorphous carbon with incomplete structure (Fig. 1e), which was mainly caused by the carbonization polymerization of citric acid and ethylenediamine during hydrothermal process [15]. The synthesis of CPD was a very complex hydrothermal process, and the reaction temperature and reaction time had important effects on the synthesis of CPD. Therefore, both amorphous and lattice structures would exist in CPD, and the lattice structures also had different phases [22]. Fig. 1d is an XRD plot of the Al2O3 raw material. It can be noted that the phase is consistent with the alumina phase numbered 77-0396 in the standard PDF card. This facilitates the calibration in TEM diagrams.

The SEM patterns of the prepared powder are provided in Fig. 2 . After reduction, the surface of MLCu was fairly flat and smooth, with a few pits on the surface and almost no pores in Fig. 2a. After adding CPD or Al2O3, fibrous structures could be observed on the surface of the matrix in Fig. 2b or d, suggesting that the reinforcements were successfully bonded to the matrix. After reduction, pores appeared on the surface of the copper, which were caused by the oxygen in CuO or Cu2O combining with hydrogen to generate water vapor. By comparing Fig. 2c and e, it can be viewed that the surface of MLCPD had more pores and it was not as smooth as MLAl2O3. This is because CPD is fluffy and easily soluble in water [22]. The pore size of the MLCPD surface was large. This might be due to the fact that the water vapor generated by the reduction brought some unbound CPD out of the sample.

The BM powder was quite different from the ML powder. In Fig. 2f, the BMCu had a smooth surface with large-shaped flake, which facilitated the adhesion of MLCu. There were few holes on the surface of BMCPD powder in Fig. 2g. Due to the properties of CPD [22], it can easily adsorb onto the Cu surface without damaging the substrate structure. It can be seen that some wheat ear shaped copper powders which had not been ground into flake powder were also easy to adsorb CPD. However, unlike BMCPD, BMAl2O3 in Fig. 2h had many holes on its surface. As Al2O3 is a much harder phase than copper [3], it can easily damage the surface of the flake copper powder. The interface wettability of the two is so poor that they are not easy to be combined together [4]. Fig. 2i demonstrates the MLAl2O3 + BMCPD powder. It can be viewed that MLAl2O3 (hundreds to thousands of nanometers) were uniformly dispersed around BMCu (tens to hundreds of microns). The disordered distribution of copper particles of different sizes played the role of micro-nano strengthening [23,24].

By analysing the prepared powders, it was found that MLCu and BMCu had different morphologies and showed more differences with the addition of CPD or Al2O3. Therefore, in this study, different powder mixing methods would be tried to prepare the composite materials. They were MLCPD + BMCPD and MLAl2O3 + BMAl2O3, which used CPD or Al2O3 alone as reinforcement, and BMCPD + BMAl2O3 and MLCPD + MLAl2O3, which used BMCu or MLCu alone as copper matrix. There were also MLCPD + BMAl2O3 and MLAl2O3 + BMCPD, which used both copper matrixes and both reinforcements. The XRD patterns of the six groups of composite powders and pure copper are shown in Fig.S1 (Fig. S1 can be seen in Supplementary material). The diffraction peaks of Al2O3 and CPD were not visible because the total addition was 0.45 wt% and the mass fraction was too low [25]. The diffraction peaks of Cu were clearly visible in all samples. The peak intensity of MLCPD + MLAl2O3 was significantly lower and the peak is wider compared to Cu. For MLCu was very fine, it was different from the flake powder prepared by ball milling [26], which matched to the SEM images. Since BMCu was a larger flake than MLCu, the peak intensity of BMCPD + BMAl2O3 became higher and narrower.

In most studies, the sintering temperature of copper matrix composites is about 800 °C [6,27]. By adding carbonaceous materials as reinforcements, the sintering temperature will be reduced [15,28]. In the study of using carbonaceous materials as reinforcement materials by molecular level mixing method, Xue [18] even used 550 °C as the sintering temperature. In this study, 650 °C was used as the sintering temperature for all samples.

The densification, conductivity, hardness and tensile properties of all samples are shown in Table 1 (related Figs. S2–S4 can be seen in Supplementary material). The densification, conductivity and hardness measured six values for each sample and took the average value to ensure the reliability of the data. For tensile testing, two tensile specimens were selected for each sample to ensure repeatability. From Table 1, all samples have a density greater than 99%, thus eliminating the effect of porosity in the later performance comparisons and making the comparisons more reliable.

As can be seen from Table 1, the conductivity of the material decreased slightly when CPD was added alone, because the carbonaceous material also has good conductivity [15]. However, the addition of Al2O3 alone resulted in a significant reduction in the conductivity of the material, from 96.1% IACS of pure copper to 90.0% IACS, which was consistent with the references [29]. In terms of hardness, CPD or Al2O3 could play the role of dispersion strengthening and fine grain strengthening [15,29]. While due to the dispersion problem, the effect of hardness improvement was similar. Both of MLCPD + BMCPD and MLAl2O3 + BMAl2O3 were increased by about 18%. Adding CPD or Al2O3 alone could enhance the tensile properties of the materials, but the effect was not very ideal. Especially after adding Al2O3, the elongation after fracture was greatly reduced, which is also consistent with the reference study [30].

In the comparison of BMCPD + BMAl2O3 and MLCPD + MLAl2O3, their conductivity were relatively similar, both were about 94% IACS. In terms of hardness, BMCPD + BMAl2O3 showed only a small increase compared with pure copper. It could be seen from the SEM image of the powder that both BMCPD and BMAl2O3 were large-sized flake powders and that Al2O3 could not be adhered very firmly [4]. It was on the surface of the flake powder and caused a certain degree of damage to the flake powder, resulting in low hardness value of the BMCPD + BMAl2O3 sample. In the MLCPD + MLAl2O3 sample, the powder particles were smaller and the uniform distribution of the nano-reinforcement phase could significantly increase the hardness of the material [31]. Compared with pure copper, the tensile properties of BMCPD + BMAl2O3 were basically the same, with only a small improvement. In contrast, MLCPD + MLAl2O3 obtained good yield strength and tensile strength, and the tensile strength even reached at 428.38 MPa. However, the elongation of MLCPD + MLAl2O3 was greatly reduced, with the elongation after fracture was only 7.91%, which is consistent with the references [18]. The elongation of the sample prepared by molecular level mixing method was very low. This is mainly due to the huge difference in the copper matrix. The smaller size of MLCu was more tightly bound than the larger size of BMCu. This can also be seen in the density of Table 1.

The conductivity of MLCPD + BMAl2O3 and MLAl2O3 + BMCPD samples decreased slightly compared with that of pure copper, still with about 94% IACS. The hardness of the two groups of samples was higher than that of pure copper, indicating that CPD and Al2O3 were well dispersed in the sample and played a role of dispersion strengthening. The MLAl2O3 + BMCPD even reached at 125 HV, which was increased by 25%. As for tensile properties, MLAl2O3 + BMCPD not only achieved excellent tensile strength (366.14 MPa) and yield strength (157 MPa), but also ensured a certain elongation (19.8%). It obtained better comprehensive mechanical properties. This is mainly due to the good combination of Al2O3 with Cu. In BMAl2O3, Al2O3 destroyed the Cu matrix to a certain extent and poorly combined with Cu. Excess NaOH was added during the preparation of MLAl2O3, so that Al2O3 was sensitized in an alkaline environment and more strongly bound to the generated CuO and CuO2 [2]. On the other hand, the CPD could be well dispersed in BMCu rather than MLCu. BMCu is a large-size flake copper powder with smooth surface, which facilitates the adhesion of CPD. Unlike BMCPD, in MLCPD the powder surface was rough and had a large number of pores, which was not conducive to the improvement of mechanical properties. For these reasons, the mechanical properties of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD were better than MLCPD + BMAl2O3.

Fig. 3 is the fracture surfaces of the tensile tested specimens. In Fig. 3a, there were many large dimples that matched the good elongation of pure copper specimen [16], and the dimples were uniform in size. In MLCPD + BMCPD specimen, the differences in dimple size were more pronounced and there were many deep dimples. In the MLAl2O3 + BMAl2O3 specimen, the dimple sizes were significantly smaller than those of the pure copper specimen, which corresponded to the poor elongation [32]. Al2O3 and CPD could play the role of staggered pinning at the appropriate locations [31], so the addition of Al2O3 and CPD together can be considered as synergistic reinforcement to improve the tensile strength. As shown in Fig. 3d, there were large cracks and obvious signs of tearing in the BMCPD + BMAl2O3 specimen, resulting in a low tensile strength. However, the overall performance was slightly higher than that of pure copper due to the strengthening effect of CPD and Al2O3. In the MLCPD + MLAl2O3 specimen, the dimples were further reduced in size and more evenly distributed. There was no dimple with larger size, which should correspond to the higher tensile strength and smaller elongation of the sample. The size distribution of the dimples in the MLCPD + BMAl2O3 specimen was significantly less uniform than that in the MLAl2O3 + BMCPD specimen.

Fig. 4 shows the TEM picture of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD sample. The EDS mapping of the yellow dotted box in Fig. 4a is shown in Fig. 4b. Therefore, in Fig. 4a, the dark area is Cu matrix and the bright spots are Al2O3 or CPD. It can be observed that the bright spots are evenly dispersed around the copper matrix.

Fig. 4c is a HRTEM image in the white dotted box of Fig. 4a. Clear lattice fringes can be observed. FFT and IFFT are performed on the three regions (d), (e), and (f). Fig. 4d shows the diffraction spots of Cu {111}. The plane spacing of the lattice measured by the IFFT image after noise filtering was 0.212 nm. The FFT image in the upper right corner of Fig. 4e shows the diffraction spots in region (e) of Fig. 4c. The diffraction spots of Al2O3 (400) and (331) could be observed. The lattice spacing measured in the corresponding IFFT image was 0.207 nm and 0.182 nm respectively. The included angle between the two sets of diffraction spots was found to be 46° and the calculated result of two sets of diffraction spots was 46.5°, which are essentially the same.

The FFT image in the upper right corner of Fig. 4f shows the diffraction spots in region (f) of Fig. 4c. The diffraction spots of Cu (200) and (111) could be observed. The lattice spacing measured in the corresponding IFFT image was 0.184 nm and 0.211 nm respectively, and the included angle between the two sets of diffraction spots was 55°, with the calculation result was 54.7°. The FFT image in the upper right corner of Fig. 4f also shows the diffraction spot of C (111) with a measured lattice plane spacing of 0.206 nm (numbered 80-0017).

The TEM images showed that there were no chemical reaction products in the bulk composite. The copper matrix, Al2O3 and CPD were tightly bonded. The CPD and Al2O3 at the grain boundaries not only acted as load transmitters but also blocked the dislocation movement, thus improving the tensile strength of the material.

Fig. 5 shows the interfacial structure of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD sample. Fig. 5a shows the HRTEM image of the interfacial structure, in which three regions (b), (c), (d) were analyzed separately. In Fig. 5b the diffraction point was identified as Cu (111) with the lattice spacing of 0.208 nm. The FFT diagram in the upper right corner of Fig. 5c showed the diffraction spots in the region (c) of Fig. 5a. Diffraction spots of Al2O3 (222) and Cu (111) could be observed, and the lattice spacing measured in the corresponding IFFT diagram were 0.229 nm and 0.212 nm respectively. By performing FFT and IFFT transformations on the region (d), Fig. 5d1 and d2 were obtained. The amorphous carbon was clearly found in the corresponding FFT figure, which was shown to facilitate the interfacial bonding and improve the mechanical properties of the material [15,33]. By measuring the angles of diffraction spots and lattice spacing, three sets of diffraction spots were identified as C(100), C(002), and C(102). They all belong to the standard PDF card of 80–0004. The other two sets of diffraction spots had lattice spacing of 0.162 nm and 0.106 nm, corresponding to Al2O3 (422) and Cu (311) respectively.

The analysis of the interfacial structure of the composite revealed the presence of Cu, Al2O3 and CPD at the interface, which was beneficial to the formation of strong interfacial bonding and thus promoting the performance of the composite.

In this study, the copper matrix composite (MLAl2O3 + BMCPD) reinforced by Al2O3 and CPD obtained better mechanical properties while maintaining high electrical conductivity. Next, the strengthening mechanism is discussed. The strengthening mechanism discussed in this experiment is expressed by Formula (1).
(1)
σ C
=
σ M
+ ∆
σ GR
+ ∆
σ LT
+ ∆
σ D
σ
C is the calculated tensile strength of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD composite, σ
M (292.72 MPa) is the matrix strength, ∆σ
GR , ∆σLT, ∆σ
D are the value of tensile strength due to grain refinement, load transfer strengthening, dislocation strengthening.

The addition of CPD and Al2O3 to copper matrix can inhibit the grain growth during sintering. With the grain refinement, the grain boundary area increased, which could better hinder the movement of dislocations and improve the strength of materials. The calculation of ∆σ
GR adopted Formula (2) [34].
(2)
∆
σ GR
= K
D
FHAl 2
O 3
+ QMC
− 0.5
−
D Cu
− 0.5
K is 0.07 MPa·m0.5 [35], D
FHAl2O3+QMC and D
Cu are the average grain size of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD and Cu. The metallographic images are shown in Fig. 6 . The average grain size of Cu and MLAl2O3 + BMCPD composites are 6.32 μm and 3.84 μm. The result shows that CPD and Al2O3 have obvious grain refinement effect. According to the above formula, the value of ∆σ
GR can be calculated as 7.88 MPa.

One strengthening mechanism of metal matrix composites is the transfer of load from matrix to the reinforcement, and the reinforcement is the main bearer of the load. ∆σ
LT can be calculated according to Formula (3) [36].
(3)
∆
σ LT
=
V f
σ my
l + t
A / 4 l
V
f is the volume fraction of reinforcement in composites, which are converted into 1.26% (CPD) and 0.879% (Al2O3). σ
my is the yield strength of pure Cu (133.76 MPa), l and t are the lengths of the reinforcement particles parallel to and perpendicular to the load direction. CPD and Al2O3 are regarded as equiaxed grains, which can be calculated by using simplified Formula (4) [36]. The calculated value is 0.93 MPa, which is due to the fact that the added CPD and Al2O3 were ultrafine particles below 10 nm with a volume fraction of less than 2%, so they played a weak role in load transfer. This is also consistent with the fact that the tensile strength of the material is improved less by adding CPD or Al2O3 alone in the previous experiment.
(4)
∆
σ LT
=
V f
σ my
/ 2

Dislocation strengthening is calculated by Williamson-Hall [37], which is based on the XRD analysis of the samples after tensile specimen. The dislocation density is estimated by Formulas (5) and (6).
(5)
βcos θ = Kλ / d + εsin θ
(6)
ρ = 2
3
ε / db
β is the FWHM of the characteristic peak in Fig. 7a, θ is the Bragg angle, b is the Burgers vector (0.256 nm [38]), K is a constant (0.89), λ is the wavelength of Kα ray of Cu target (0.154 nm), Fig. 7b is a linear fitting based on XRD data, the slope is ε, and the longitudinal intercept of fitting line is d, so as to calculate the dislocation density ρ of Cu and MLAl2O3 + BMCPD. The final results show that the dislocation density of Cu is 7.28 × 1014 m −2. The dislocation density of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD is 9.99 × 1014 m −2, indicating that the addition of CPD and Al2O3 can effectively improve the dislocation density of the composites. The ∆σ
D can be calculated according to Formula (7).
(7)
∆
σ D
= ∆
σ CD
− ∆
σ MD
= αGb
ρ C
0.5
−
ρ m
0.5
ρ
C and ρ
m are the dislocation density of MLAl2O3 + BMCPD and Cu respectively, G is the shear modulus (42.1 GPa [39]), α is a constant (1.25), and the final calculated ∆σ
D is 62.31 MPa.

All enhancement effects are summarized in Fig. 8 , with a calculated theoretical value of 363.84 MPa, which is very close to the experimental value (366.14 MPa), proving the effectiveness of the strengthening mechanism. From the summary data, it can be seen that the improvement of composite strength is mainly due to dislocation strengthening and the addition of CPD and Al2O3 can play a good role in pinning dislocations.

A novel copper-based composite material synergistically strengthened by Al2O3 and CPD was successfully prepared by SPS using different mixing methods. The effect of the mixing methods of reinforcements and metal matrix on microstructure, density, conductivity, hardness and tensile properties were studied. The conclusions are summarized as follows:
(1)
Al2O3 is suitable for mixing with copper powder by molecular-level method. When Al2O3 and BMCu are combined, Al2O3 will cause some damage to the flake copper powder, and the interface wettability between them is poor. Due to the sensitization effect of NaOH, there is a tight bonding between Al2O3 and MLCu.
(2)
CPD is suitable for mixing with copper powder by ball milling method. When CPD and MLCu are combined, there are larger pores, which may cause the loss of CPD. When CPD is combined with BMCu, it is easy to be adsorbed on the surface of BMCu without damaging the structure of BMCu.
(3)
MLAl2O3 + BMCPD composite prepared by the combination of CPD with BMCu and Al2O3 with MLCu has the best properties. The conductivity is 93.15% IACS, the hardness is 124.66 HV, and the yield strength and tensile strength are 157.00 MPa and 366.14 MPa respectively. Compared with pure copper, the electrical conductivity is not significantly reduced, but the mechanical properties are greatly improved. The strength of composite is mainly due to dislocation strengthening. Compared with MLCPD + BMCPD and MLAl2O3 + BMAl2O3 samples, CPD and Al2O3 in MLAl2O3 + BMCPD sample have synergistic strengthening effect.
Haichuan Luo: data curation; writing – original draft
Rui Bao: review & editing; resources.
Ruofei Ma: measurements.
Jiachen Liu: measurements.
Youliang Nie: measurements.
Jian-hong Yi: resources; supervision.
All the authors have reviewed, discussed and approved the results and conclusions of this manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52064032, 52174345), the Yunnan science and technology projects (Grant No. 202002AB080001), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005).
The data used to support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon request.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.108916.
